1: The burden of the Valley of Vision. Massa -- the oracle-burden, now applied to Jerusalem itself. The Valley of Vision is associated with Jerusalem (the Hinnom Valley or the broader depression around the city). The irony is acute: Jerusalem, the place of divine vision, has become the object of the oracle of judgment. The scene of the people on rooftops celebrating while surrounded by siege fits either the Assyrian threat (701 BC) or a later Babylonian siege.
2: You are a city full of uproar and celebration! The Hebrew mehuma (tumult) and aliza (exultant) depict a city in festive mood despite military crisis. The shocking note: your slain were not killed by the sword -- they died not heroically in battle but from famine, disease, and flight. Not a warrior's death but the disgrace of non-combat death in siege conditions. The MKT preserves the distinction.
3: All your commanders fled together. Military leadership's flight without battle -- captured without a bow being drawn. The bow (keshet) as synecdoche for military resistance: not a single bow was drawn in defense. The commanders were found and taken by archers in their flight -- captured, not defeated. The dishonor of this kind of surrender is what Isaiah mourns in v4.
4: Isaiah refuses to be comforted: Turn away from me; let me weep bitterly. The prophet personally mourns over Jerusalem's ruin -- he is not detached from the people he indicts. Pastoral lamentation alongside prophetic judgment: both are authentic responses to the same reality. The personal voice (I said, let me weep bitterly) makes this more than oracle; it is private grief at covenant failure.
5: The double divine title Adonai YHWH-Sabaoth -- Master + covenant name + army commander -- governs the day of panic. The three nouns for what fills the Valley: mehuma (panic), mirmas (trampling), mevukha (confusion) -- alliterative in Hebrew. The battering of walls and crying to the mountains fills the day with disaster from every direction.
6: Elam and Kir -- two nations used as Assyrian auxiliaries -- contribute to the siege. Elam's quiver and Kir's shield represent different military units: archery and close-combat infantry. The specificity of military equipment (quiver, chariot, cavalry, shield) gives the oracle historical density. Jerusalem is surrounded by the composite forces of the Assyrian imperial army.
7: The finest valleys choked with chariots and cavalry posted at the gates -- the siege of the city from the surrounding terrain. The finest valleys are productive agricultural land now turned into a military staging area. Cavalry at the city gates means no one enters or exits -- the classic siege cut-off. The MKT's choked with chariots captures the sense of overwhelming density.
8: He stripped away Judah's defenses. The masculine singular agent -- the LORD himself -- removed Judah's covering on that day. The people's response was to look to the human arsenal (Solomon's armory, the House of the Forest, 1 Kings 7:2) rather than to the LORD. When divine protection is withdrawn, the people's instinct is to trust human military resources rather than to return to the one who withdrew the protection.
9: The technical details of Jerusalem's water system -- the gaps in the wall, the lower pool -- are consistent with Hezekiah's Siloam tunnel (2 Kings 20:20; 2 Chron 32:30). The preparations are practically competent. But v11 will indict the failure behind all this competent preparation: they looked to the human water system and not to the one who fashioned it long ago.
10: Taking inventory of the houses and demolishing buildings to shore up the wall -- a standard siege-preparation practice. Jerusalem's response to the threat is technically rational: census the population, mobilize the housing, strengthen the walls. The problem is not the tactics but the theology: all this human preparation occurs without returning to the LORD whose word determines the outcome.
11: But you did not look to him who made it. The accusation is not against the practical preparations but against the direction of trust. The two pools, the wall, the cistern -- all created by the same LORD who fashioned the land long ago. The MKT's you did not look to him who made it and you did not see him who planned it long ago preserves the two Hebrew verbs of spiritual perception that Jerusalem failed to exercise.
12: YHWH-Sabaoth called for weeping, mourning, shaved heads, and sackcloth -- the standard signs of grief and repentance in a crisis. The divine expectation was penitential return. What actually happened (v13) is the opposite: celebration and feasting. The contrast between what the LORD called for and what the people chose is the moral center of the oracle against Jerusalem.
13: Instead there was only celebration -- slaughtering oxen, killing sheep, eating meat and drinking wine. Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die. The feast of denial: present-tense verbs of consuming, then the fatalistic refrain. Paul cites this verse in 1 Cor 15:32 as the logical conclusion of a theology without resurrection: if the dead are not raised, hedonistic fatalism is the rational response. The MKT's exact phrasing matches the form Paul cites.
14: YHWH is flagged -- the LORD revealed this directly in the prophet's hearing: This guilt will never be atoned for while you live. The irrevocability is unusual in Isaiah, where most oracles of judgment are conditional or reversible. The phrase niglah be-aznai (uncovered/revealed in my ears) conveys intimate auditory revelation. The oracle carries the weight of a final refusal of repentance, beyond which further intercession is pointless.
15: The oracle shifts from the people collectively to Shebna, the palace administrator (asher al-habayit: the one over the house -- the chief steward). The abrupt targeting of a specific bureaucrat is unusual prophetic practice. Shebna is accused of carving a grand tomb for himself -- claiming the honor of the royal burial ground for a man of no distinguished lineage. The divine commissioning formula (Go) makes this a direct divine charge.
16: What gives you the right to be here? Who do you think you are? The blunt rhetorical challenge -- not even naming Shebna, addressing him as this man -- drips with prophetic contempt. The tomb was carved in the rock on a height, the most prestigious burial location. The MKT's what gives you the right captures the Hebrew mah-lekha poh literally: what is to you here? The implied answer: nothing you have earned.
17: YHWH is flagged -- the LORD is about to throw Shebna away violently. The metaphor of hurling -- tavhevkha: whirl you around and throw you -- is physical and humiliating. The violent throw of a large man from a high position. The MKT's hurl you away with a violent throw captures the forceful Hebrew. The promised honor of a grand tomb is inverted: Shebna will die in a foreign land.
18: YHWH is flagged; kavod (glory, disp 2) -- your glorious chariots will be the shame of your master's house. The kavod that Shebna sought through his grand tomb and ceremonial chariots becomes the very vehicle of his shame. The MKT's glorious chariots renders kavod's range from physical splendor to proud status. Outward display of honor becomes the marker of disgrace when expelled.
19: The removal oracle is stated plainly: I will remove you from your position, and you will be pulled down from your office. The MKT uses the pair remove / pulled down to translate the Hebrew double verb of displacement. Both the status-role and the functional-role are taken away. The displacement creates the vacancy that Eliakim (v20) is called to fill.
20: Eliakim son of Hilkiah is summoned. The contrast with Shebna is implicit: Shebna's family is unspecified; Eliakim's father Hilkiah is named. The divine first-person commissioning (I will summon my servant Eliakim) makes this a divine appointment, not a political promotion. The word servant (avdi: my servant) is the title of honor given to Moses, David, and the Servant of the LORD in chs 40-55 -- Eliakim participates in this honorific vocabulary.
21: The investiture of Eliakim with Shebna's robe, sash, and authority is a formal succession ceremony. Eliakim becomes a father to the residents of Jerusalem and to the house of Judah -- the pastoral-paternal role of the chief steward. The MKT's hand your authority over to him is appropriately formal for an act of official investiture, and the paternal title underscores the protective, not merely administrative, dimension of the office.
22: I will lay on his shoulder the key of the house of David; what he opens no one will shut, and what he shuts no one will open. The key of the house of David is the chief steward's authority over royal access. Rev 3:7 quotes this verse directly for Christ: who holds the key of David, what he opens no one can shut, and what he shuts no one can open. The MKT's exact phrasing closely follows the Hebrew and is quotable for Rev 3:7 without adjustment.
23: I will drive him in like a peg into a solid place, and he will become a throne of glory to his ancestral household. Aman (disp 2) is the root of solid place -- the same root as emet (truth/faithfulness) and amen. The maqom ne'eman (solid/faithful/reliable place) secures the peg in something genuinely trustworthy. Kavod (glory, disp 2) -- Eliakim will become a throne of glory. The MKT's solid place captures aman's reliability sense without importing the belief sense.
24: Everything of the household will hang on Eliakim -- all the kavod (honor/glory, disp 2), from the largest vessels to the smallest bowls. The MKT's all the honour of his father's household captures the kavod that transfers to Eliakim's peg. The list of vessels hanging on the peg illustrates how much weight a single trustworthy appointment can bear -- and therefore how catastrophic its failure would be (v25).
25: In that day the peg that was driven into the solid place will fail. Aman (disp 2) appears again -- the solid/faithful place gives way and the peg is cut down and falls, and everything hanging on it falls with it. YHWH is flagged twice. Even a divinely appointed steward is temporary. Only the one who holds the key of David permanently (Rev 3:7 -- Christ) is the true and unbreakable peg -- the fulfillment that makes all human stewards types and shadows.
